text: The surgeon releases the lateral attachment of the uterus by coagulating and cutting the left round ligament, enhancing the mobility of the uterus and improving the visualization of the left adnexa.

The surgeon releases the tube and utero-ovarian ligament, with assistance retracting the left ovary to clearly expose the utero-ovarian ligament. The ligament is controlled by coagulation and cutting, revealing the lateral face of the uterus. The brown ligament is opened, and the insertion of the left uterosacral ligament is controlled by dissecting the left part of the brown ligament.

The surgeon coagulates and cuts the right round ligament, enhancing the mobility of the uterus, which is then moved laterally to the left. Subsequently, the surgeon performs vesicle uterine mobilization by incising the peritoneum between the bladder and the uterus, revealing a substantial amount of connective tissue.

The surgeon removes tissue between the bladder and vagina to expose the vesico-uterine ligament, cutting it at the cranial and medial parts until the anterior part of the uterine manipulator is visible. The same procedure is performed on the left side, cutting the vesico-uterine ligament at its median part to avoid ureter damage.

The surgeon controls the left uterine artery, a large vessel, ensuring careful management to prevent accidental bleeding. The vein is cut, and dissection proceeds posteriorly to reveal the posterior valve of the uterine manipulator. The next step involves cutting the right fallopian tube and the right utero-ovarian ligament.
The surgeon uses bipolar energy to coagulate a large vascular ligament, ensuring hemostasis over a span of two to three centimeters to prevent accidental bleeding.

The surgeon locates the right uterine pedicle and removes connective tissues between the uterus and adnexa, ensuring not to damage a large vein. Coagulation is applied to the uterine arteries and cervico-vaginal vessel on the right side of the uterus over two to three centimeters. The cervico-vaginal vein is cut, followed by the cervical vaginal vessel on the right side.

The surgeon performs a colpotomy using a monopolar needle to cut the vagina on the uterine manipulator. Under direct vision, the left, anterior, and right parts of the vagina are incised. The monopolar needle is repositioned on the left trocar to achieve a perpendicular angle for cutting the posterior part of the vagina. The right uterosacral ligament is also cut during this process.

The surgeon performs morcellation of a large uterus in the abdomen using a chardonnay knife, carefully cutting the cervix and dividing the uterus into two pieces under visual control. Vaginal forceps are applied to the hemi-uterus, which is then extracted vaginally without damaging the surrounding tissues.

The surgeon removes the second part of the uterus under visual control, ensuring no damage to the vagina. The procedure is completed quickly, and the surgeon begins suturing the edge of the vagina, starting laterally.

The surgeon sutures the left part of the vagina, incorporating peritoneal tissue, using extracorporeal knotting with a 40mm needle and zero vicryl. The entire vaginal mucosa is included in the suture, and the closure is completed with an X knot. The same technique is applied to the right part of the vagina, ensuring a secure closure with multiple knots.
